skill.name: python-async-patterns
skill.description: Guidance and examples for building reliable Python asyncio applications, including concurrency, aiohttp, error handling, debugging, and production patterns.
skill.summary: Apply practical asyncio patterns for concurrent Python services and tools.
skill.author: 0xDarkMatter

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many alerts, but manual review found they are mostly markdown code fences, instructional aiohttp examples, and a user-invoked ripgrep helper script. No prompt injection, credential access, obfuscation, hidden exfiltration, or confirmed weak cryptography was found. The skill is safe to publish with a low-risk warning for copied network, filesystem, and script examples.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The many Ruby or shell backtick alerts are markdown code fences and inline code examples in instructional files. They do not execute commands by themselves, and no command injection path was found in those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line inspection shows fenced Python examples rather than runtime command execution. This strongly supports a false positive for the external command alerts.

low_findings.title: Instructional Network Examples
low_findings.description: The skill contains aiohttp examples and a template that fetch caller-supplied URLs or httpbin demonstration URLs. No credential exfiltration, hidden endpoint, or automatic network execution was found, but copied examples can make outbound requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network calls are visible examples aligned with the asyncio and aiohttp purpose of the skill. They still deserve a low warning because users may run the template unchanged.

low_findings.title: Instructional File Write Example
low_findings.description: A documentation example downloads response content to a caller-provided destination path. This is not active malicious behavior, but production use should validate paths and avoid overwriting sensitive files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file write appears only inside a documented file download pattern. The risk is low because execution requires user adoption, but the path handling concern is real if copied directly.

low_findings.title: User-Invoked Local Scan Script
low_findings.description: The repository includes a bash helper that runs ripgrep searches against a user-selected directory. The directory argument is quoted and the commands are fixed, so no command injection evidence was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script performs local static searches and does not download code, read secrets intentionally, or execute user-provided command fragments. It is still a script risk factor because users can run it locally.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts point to markdown text and code-fence context, not to MD5, SHA1, DES, or another weak cryptographic API. No evidence of cryptographic misuse was found at the flagged locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines shows ordinary prose and async file download examples. The static signatures do not match actual weak cryptographic operations.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Not Found
low_findings.description: Targeted review found no text telling the evaluator to ignore instructions, skip analysis, claim pre-approval, or assume special authority. No prompt-injection evidence was found in the scanned skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: A targeted search for common prompt-injection phrases returned no matches. This supports dismissing the critical heuristic combination as unconfirmed.

content.value_statement: Async Python code can become fragile when tasks, timeouts, and blocking calls are handled inconsistently. This skill provides focused asyncio patterns for safer concurrency, HTTP clients, retries, debugging, and production cleanup.
actual_capabilities: Explains asyncio task patterns, including gather, TaskGroup, semaphores, queues, locks, and timeouts.
Provides aiohttp client and server examples for sessions, uploads, downloads, WebSockets, and bounded requests.
Shows retry, backoff, circuit breaker, and partial failure handling patterns for async workloads.
Documents debugging and profiling approaches for leaked tasks, blocked event loops, and slow coroutines.
Includes a reusable async application template with lifecycle management and graceful shutdown.
Includes a shell helper that scans Python projects for common blocking calls inside async code.
limitations: Examples are instructional and may need security, validation, and error handling changes before production use.
Some patterns require Python 3.11 features such as TaskGroup and asyncio.timeout.
The shell helper depends on ripgrep and only uses pattern matching, so it can miss semantic blocking issues.
The skill does not automatically inspect live services, run benchmarks, or verify external API behavior.

use_cases.title: Modernize Blocking Python Code
use_cases.description: Replace blocking sleeps, HTTP calls, subprocess calls, and file operations with async-aware alternatives.
use_cases.target_user: Backend engineers migrating synchronous Python services

use_cases.title: Design Concurrent HTTP Workflows
use_cases.description: Build bounded aiohttp request flows with shared sessions, retries, timeouts, and partial failure handling.

use_cases.title: Debug Async Production Issues
use_cases.description: Investigate task leaks, pending tasks, event loop stalls, and slow coroutine behavior using documented patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Explain Async Basics
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of an asyncio function
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain how this async Python function works. Identify what must be awaited, what can run concurrently, and what could block the event loop.

prompt_templates.title: Convert Sync Code
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this synchronous Python workflow and propose an asyncio version. Use bounded concurrency, clear timeout handling, and safe resource cleanup.

prompt_templates.title: Harden HTTP Concurrency
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an aiohttp client workflow for these endpoints. Include session lifecycle, concurrency limits, retries with backoff, partial failures, and observability points.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Production Async Code
prompt_templates.scenario: Expert review of production reliability risks
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this async Python module for event loop blocking, leaked tasks, cancellation problems, missing timeouts, unsafe shared state, and shutdown gaps.

output_examples.input: A developer asks why several awaited HTTP calls are still slow.
output_examples.output: The response explains sequential awaits, recommends bounded concurrent tasks, and notes where timeout and retry policies should be added.

output_examples.input: A team shares an async service that does not exit cleanly.
output_examples.output: The response identifies missing task tracking, incomplete cancellation handling, and session cleanup steps needed during shutdown.

output_examples.input: A maintainer wants to find blocking calls in async functions.
output_examples.output: The response describes using the included scan helper, then manually reviewing matches for context and false positives.

best_practices: Set explicit timeouts and cancellation paths for every network or long-running async operation.
Use shared client sessions and bounded concurrency instead of creating unbounded tasks.
Review generated examples before production use, especially paths, URLs, credentials, and retry behavior.
anti_patterns: Do not call blocking libraries such as requests or time.sleep inside async functions.
Do not create background tasks without retaining references and handling cancellation.
Do not use unbounded gather calls for large input lists or external service fan-out.

faq.question: Does this skill execute async code automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides documentation, examples, an optional template, and a local scan script for user-controlled use.

faq.question: Which Python versions are supported?
faq.answer: Python 3.10 is the baseline, but TaskGroup and asyncio.timeout examples require Python 3.11 or newer.

faq.question: Can it help with aiohttp clients?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers shared sessions, connection limits, timeouts, retries, uploads, downloads, and WebSocket examples.

faq.question: Can it detect every blocking call?
faq.answer: No. The helper script uses pattern matching, so human review is still required for accurate findings.

faq.question: Is the included project template production ready as-is?
faq.answer: It is a strong starting point, but teams should adapt logging, configuration, security controls, and external endpoints.

faq.question: Does it handle database patterns?
faq.answer: It includes basic async database examples and points toward async drivers, but it is not a full database design guide.
